coredata.dc:title: An “on-off” signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of dual microRNAs based on DNAzyme-powered DNA walker
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Li
Zhao, Kai-Ren
Liu, Zhi-Jun
Zhang, Yi-Bang
Liu, Peng-Fei
Ye, Shu-Ying
Zhang, Ye-Wang
Liang, Guo-Xi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, an “on-off” signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for dual miRNAs (miRNA-21 and miRNA-155) detection was constructed based on carbon dots (CDs)-labeled DNA walker, which can effectively avoid problems of the signal overlap or limited available labels that general exist in multiplex assays. In the presence of miRNA-21, the CDs-DNA walker were captured on the electrode via nucleic acid hybridization, producing a strong ECL signal corresponding to miRNA-21 (signal on). For miRNA-155, the biosensor was pre-incubated with CDs-DNA walker and assistant DNA (DNA-21). Target miRNA-155 could trigger metal ion-dependent DNAzyme cleavage. With the assistance of Mn2+, the substrate strands were cleaved and CDs detached from the electrode, resulting in a reduced ECL signal (signal off). The interference of miRNA-21 was eliminated because no more CDs-DNA walker was added. As a result, benefiting from the signal amplification offered by the CDs-DNA walker and the inherent properties of ECL technique, this biosensor achieved low detection limits of 33 fM for miRNA-21 and 33 aM for miRNA-155. This paper also demonstrates the biosensor’s bioanalytical application for multiplex detection of miRNA-21/155 in human serum samples and the total RNA extraction solution from the human cancer cell lines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiplex detection
DNA walker
Electrochemiluminescence
miRNA-21
miRNA-155

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Feasibility of the proposed sensing platform
Analytical ability of the ECL biosensor in miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) PAGE characterization of the CP1/miRNA-21/CP2 hybridization. Lane 1: CP2; lane 2: CP1; lane 3: miRNA-21; lane 4: mixture of CP1 and miRNA-21; lane 5: mixture of CP1, CP2 and miRNA-21; lane 6: mixture of CP2 and miRNA-21. (B) PAGE characterization of the miRNA-155-triggered DNAzyme activation. Lane 1: miRNA-155; lane 2: locking strand; lane 3: DNAzyme strand; lane 4: mixture of locking strand and miRNA-155; lane 5: mixture of locking strand and DNAzyme strand; lane 6: mixture of locking strand–DNAzyme strand complex and miRNA-155. Concentration of every component used in PAGE, CP1: 5 μL 1 µM, CP2: 5 μL 1 µM, miRNA-21: 5 μL 1 µM, DNAzyme strand: 5 μL 1 µM, locking strand: 5 μL 3 µM, miRNA-155: 5 μL 1 µM. The incubation time of DNA: 2 h. (C) ECL responses of biosensor without target, with target miRNA-21, and with target miRNA-155. (D) EIS characterization of the biosensor fabrication process: (a) bare GE, (b) CP1/GE, (c) MCH/CP1/GE, (d) CDs-DNA walker/miRNA-21/MCH/CP1/GE, and (e) miRNA-155/CDs-DNA walker/miRNA-21/MCH/CP1/GE. Concentration of every component used in ECL and EIS characterization, CP1: 10 μL 0.5 µM, miRNA-21: 5 μL 50 nM, 5 μL CDs-DNA walkers, miRNA-155: 5 μL 50 pM, DNA walker reaction temperature and time: 37 °C and 60 min, respectively.

(A) ECL responses of the biosensor responding to different miRNA-21 concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 nM. (B) Linear correlation between the ECL signal and the logarithm of miRNA-21 concentrations ranging from 100 fM to 100 nM. (C) ECL responses of the biosensor responding to different miRNA-155 concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 pM. (D) Linear correlation between the ECL signal and the logarithm of miRNA-155 concentrations ranging from 100 aM to 100 pM.
Analysis of (A) miRNA-21 and (B) miRNA-155 from MCF-7 cell line: (a) 0 cell; (b) 500 cells; (c) 1000 cells; and (d) 30,000 cancer cells.

Schematic illustrations of (A) the preparation of CDs-DNA walker, (B) the principle of DNA walker-based “on-off” ECL biosensor for quantitation of miRNA-21 and miRNA-155, (C) the operation of DNA walker initiated by miRNA-155.
Comparison of our work with previous reported works for dual miRNAs determination.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DPV: deferential pulse voltammetry; NFCM: nano-flow cytometer; SERS: surface-enhanced Raman scattering.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Using the ECL biosensor to detect miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 in normal human serum.

head.ce:title: An “on-off” signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of dual microRNAs based on DNAzyme-powered DNA walker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, an “on-off” signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for dual miRNAs (miRNA-21 and miRNA-155) detection was constructed based on carbon dots (CDs)-labeled DNA walker, which can effectively avoid problems of the signal overlap or limited available labels that general exist in multiplex assays. In the presence of miRNA-21, the CDs-DNA walker were captured on the electrode via nucleic acid hybridization, producing a strong ECL signal corresponding to miRNA-21 (signal on). For miRNA-155, the biosensor was pre-incubated with CDs-DNA walker and assistant DNA (DNA-21). Target miRNA-155 could trigger metal ion-dependent DNAzyme cleavage. With the assistance of Mn2+, the substrate strands were cleaved and CDs detached from the electrode, resulting in a reduced ECL signal (signal off). The interference of miRNA-21 was eliminated because no more CDs-DNA walker was added. As a result, benefiting from the signal amplification offered by the CDs-DNA walker and the inherent properties of ECL technique, this biosensor achieved low detection limits of 33 fM for miRNA-21 and 33 aM for miRNA-155. This paper also demonstrates the biosensor’s bioanalytical application for multiplex detection of miRNA-21/155 in human serum samples and the total RNA extraction solution from the human cancer cell lines.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are a kind of endogenous non-coding small RNAs, which play prominent roles in many biological processes including cell proliferation, differentiation, apoptosis and death [1–4]. Studies have shown that some miRNAs are closely related to a variety of tumors [5,6]. Hence, they may be used as effective biomarkers for cancer diagnostics and screening [7–10]. However, it is still a challenge to accurate and unbiased detection of miRNAs because of their small sizes, high sequence homology, low abundances, and susceptibility to degradation [11]. Moreover, most cancers are associated with more than one biomarker [12–14]. Therefore, it is of great value to develop effective methods to detect multiple tumor-related miRNAs for cancer diagnosis and therapy.

Currently, one of the most commonly methods for detection of miRNA is quantitative reverse-transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) [15]. But this method requires a relatively expensive thermal cycler to precisely control the temperature. The small size of miRNAs also makes the qRT-PCR design very sophisticated [16,17]. To address the above-mentioned difficulties, some novel methods, for instance, surface plasmon resonance [18,19], fluorescence [20,21], electrochemistry [22,23] and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [24,25] have been developed. It is noteworthy that effective nucleic acid signal amplification strategies such as rolling circle amplification [26], hybridization chain reaction [27] strand displacement amplification [24,28] are almost necessary in these methods since the absolute abundance of miRNAs is extremely low.

DNA walker, a class of recently developed dynamic DNA nanomachine, has also been explored due to its excellent nucleic acid signal amplification capability, low cost, facile design and simple operation [29]. Typically, a DNA walker will autonomously and progressively move along the predesigned DNA tracks under target stimuli, and the signal copies will be accumulated during the walking process, endowing the analytical methods with highly sensitive detection capability. For example, Le’s group developed a DNAzyme-powered DNA walker for imaging miRNA-155 in living cells with a linear range from 5 to 200 pM [30]. Yu’s group proposed an electrochemical biosensor by construction the whole DNA walker on a gold electrode, allowing the detection of target DNA down to 36 fM [31], Our group also developed an ultrasensitive ECL biosensor for miRNA-155 via combining the catalytic hairpin assembly circuit and DNA walker with a limit of detection (LOD) of 4 aM [32]. However, most walkers that have been reported so far were limited to single target sensing, few of them were performed for multiplex miRNAs detection [33,34]. Difficulties may come from the possible signal overlap, the complexity of optimizing labeling conditions, and the limited number of labels available for multiplex assays [35].

Herein, we proposed a signal-switchable ECL biosensor based on the DNA walker for detection of dual miRNAs, in which the single ECL labels were used to avoid the possible problems of above-mentioned. Moreover, ECL has distinct advantages such as high sensitivity, wide dynamic range, low background signal, and simple equipment [36,37]. As a result, benefited from the signal amplification offered by the DNA walker and the inherent properties of ECL analytical technique, low detection limits of 33 fM for miRNA-21 and 33 aM for miRNA-155 were achieved. In addition, compared with standard qRT-PCR, this method has the merits of low cost, facile design and simple operation. Scheme 1 illustrates the principle of the proposed ECL biosensor. The DNA walker was prepared by functionalization of an Au nanoparticle (AuNP) with hundreds of substrate strands, dozens of capture DNA 2 strands (CP2) and dozens of locked-DNAzyme molecules that were each silenced by a locking strand according to the literature [30]. Carbon dots (CDs), is a promising ECL luminophore, due to the properties of low toxicity, low cost, high stability [38]. Here, the formed DNA walker as a tags-carrier could load lots of ECL luminescent reagent carbon dots (CDs) through ECD/NHS reaction between CDs and substrate strands, forming the CDs-DNA walker (Scheme 1A). The electrode was modified with capture DNA 1 (CP1) in advance as shown in Scheme 1B. In the presence of miRNA-21, CDs-DNA walker was connected to the modified electrode by formation of a sandwich structure via specific nucleic acid hybridization between miRNA-21, CP1 and CP2, producing a specific ECL signal corresponding to miRNA-21 (the signal is “on”). At this time, since Mn2+ was not added to the system, the subsequent DNA walker could not work, so miRNA-155 did not affect the detection of miRNA-21. For target miRNA-155 detection, a certain amount of DNA-21 and CDs-DNA walker were used to pre-treat the biosensor. With the addition of the miRNA-155, it bound to the locking strand and active the DNAzyme of DNA walker by toehold-mediated strand displacement. Subsequently, the activated DNAzyme automatically grasps substrates. In the presence of cofactor Mn2+, which can used for the cleavage between residues of substrates along the AuNPs [39], making CDs leave away from the electrode, thereby producing a decreased ECL signal (the signal is “off”). Scheme 1C is the detailed illustration of the walking process. Note that the interference of miRNA-21 on miRNA-155 could be avoided because no more CDs-DNA walker was added. Thus, by measuring the “on-off” ECL intensity change, the proposed ECL biosensor could provide an effective tool for the precise analysis of cancer biomarkers miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 and has potential application in clinical disease diagnosis.

The 20 nm-AuNPs was synthesized following the procedures reported in the literature [27]. And the CDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea by a microwave-assistant method, detailed procedures can be found in our previous work [40].

The DNA walker was first constructed by functionalizing AuNPs with locked-DNAzyme, substrate strand, and CP2 via AuS bond through a frozen method. Briefly, the DNAzyme walking strand (2 μM) and the locking strand (6 μM) were mixed in a Tris-HCl buffer, heated at 95 °C for 5 min, and cooled down to room temperature to obtain the locked-DNAzyme. Then, a mixture containing 2.5 μL of locked-DNAzyme (2 μM), 50 μL of substrate strand (2 μM), 2.5 μL of CP2 (2 μM), and 100 μL of AuNPs was frozen at − 20 °C for 2 h to obtain DNA functionalized AuNPs (DNA walker) suspension solution [41]. After that, 50 μL of the CDs containing EDC (40 mM) and NHS (10 mM), was added to above DNA walker solution and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The mixture was centrifuged (7000 rpm, 10 min) to remove the excess reagents, and the final precipitate was washed and dispersed in 100 μL of 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4 containing 0.05% Tween 20) for further use.

The working electrode (GE) was polished and cleaned with reference to the reported works [38]. After being rinsed with ultrapure water and dried in air, 10 μL of capture DNA 1 (CP1) was dropped on the electrode surface and kept at − 20 °C for 2 h to immobilize CP1 on the electrode. Next, the blocking reagent MCH (1 mM) was incubated with the modified electrode. After washing, the prepared biosensor was stored at 4 °C before use.

For miRNA-21 detection, a mixture containing 5 μL of CDs-DNA walkers and 5 μL different concentrations of miRNA-21 was dropped on the surface of the biosensor and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. For miRNA-155 detection, the prepared biosensor was first incubated with 5 μL of 10 μM assistant DNA (DNA-21) and 5 μL of CDs-DNA walkers for 2 h. After washed with water, 5 μL different concentrations of miRNA-155 and 5 μL of 0.75 mM Mn2+ were dropped and incubated at room temperature for 60 min. After washing, the biosensor was put in the ECL solution for measurement. All ECL measurements were performed in 8 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 8.0, containing 1 mM K2S2O8). The PMT was set at 800 V and potential scanning was from − 1.6 to 0 V, respectively.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of AuNPs and CDs were shown in Fig. S1A and B. As shown, both CDs and AuNPs were spherical and well dispersed, the diameter of CDs was less than 10 nm, and AuNPs is about 20 nm, together with the surface plasmon resonance absorption peak at 526 nm of AuNPs (Fig. S1D) and the strong fluorescence emission at 527 nm of CDs (Fig. S1E), the formation of CDs and AuNPs nanoparticles was proved. Fig. S1F depicted the FT-IR spectrum of CDots, which showed stretching vibrations of O-H at 3410 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups at 1680 and 1590 cm−1. The formation of CDs-DNA walker was first confirmed by TEM (Fig. S1C), in which both CDs and AuNPs could be observed. The synthesis of CDs-DNA walker was also confirmed by UV–vis absorption spectra, in which characteristic peaks of CDs (420 nm), DNA (260 nm) and AuNPs (526 nm) were all observed (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the proposed sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the validity of DNA walker-based dual-detection strategy, polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) and ECL characterizations were performed. PAGE image of Fig. 1A verified the binding of miRNA-21 with CP1 and CP2. As shown, the migration speed of CP1/miRNA-21/CP2 mixture (lane 5) was lower than that of the single DNA strands (lane 1, lane 2, lane 3) and the other CP1/miRNA-21, CP2/miRNA-21 complexes (lane 4 and lane 6), which is direct evidence for miRNA-21 successful connection of CP2 and CP1. To further prove that miRNA-155 can initiate the DNA walker, the DNA displacement reaction between miRNA-155 and locked-DNAzyme were charactered. As shown in Fig. 1B, the first three lanes (lane 1, lane 2 and lane 3) indicated miRNA-155, locking strand and DNAzyme, respectively. Here, the two bands of DNAzyme appeared (in lane 3) may because the DNAzyme dimers formed through disulfide bonds. In the absence of miRNA-155, DNAzyme was locked successfully by the locking strand (lane 5). After adding miRNA-155, a new band appeared at the bottom of lane 6, which corresponds to the targeted duplex (lane 4), and a band corresponding to DNAzyme was also observed, proving the successful activation of DNAzyme.

The ECL profiles of the dual-target detection were shown in Fig. 1C. Initially, the biosensor had no ECL response without any target. After incubation of miRNA-21 and CDs-DNA walker, significantly enhanced ECL intensity was observed compared to that of bare GE owing to the CDs-walker being connected (state-on). The ECL mechanism of CDs could be explained as follows [38]:
(1) CDs+e-→CDs-
(2) S2O4 2-+e-→SO4 2-+SO4 -
(3) 2SO4 -+CDs-→2SO4 2-+CDs*+
(4) CDs*++e-→CDs+hv

When miRNA-155 and Mn2+ were coated on the CDs-DNA walker/miRNA-21 pre-treated biosensor, a significantly decreased ECL intensity was obtained (state-off), proving the successful process of DNA walker. All PAGE and ECL results could prove that this dual-target detection strategy was feasible.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution to verify the construction process of the biosensor. The semicircle diameter in the impedance spectrum is the reflection of charge transfer resistance (Ret), the smaller radius indicates higher charge transfer rates between working electrode and electrolyte solution [42]. As shown in Fig. 1D, bare GE (Ret = 210 Ω, curve a) showed a small Ret with a tiny circle. After CP1, MCH, and CDs-DNA walker/miRNA-21 were successive incubated on GE, the Ret values were consecutively increased in order (Ret = 500 Ω, curves b; Ret = 670 Ω, curves c and Ret = 920 Ω, curves d) due to the fact that the negatively charged DNA and nonconductive MCH can prevent the electron transfer. When miRNA-155 and Mn2+ were added, the Ret further increased (about 1250 Ω, curve e), which could be explained that CDs as electron transfer promoters have successfully left the electrode [43,44]. These results suggested the assembly process on the surface of the gold electrode was consistent with the designed principle.

The density of the CP1 on the electrode was first optimized because it directly affects the performance of the biosensor. As shown in Fig. S3A, the ECL intensity gradually increased from 0.1 µM to 0.5 µM and reached plateaus after 0.5 µM. Thus, 0.5 µM would be selected as the optimal concentration of CP1 for the capture of miRNA-21 and CDs-DNA walker. The number of substrate strand on AuNPs also influenced the detection performance of the biosensor. Therefore, the different quantity of substrate strand was ordinally loaded on the same concentration AuNPs surface through Au-S bond to investigate the loading rate of substrate strand. With the increase of concentration of substrate strand, the ECL intensity increased correspondingly and reached the maximize at 2 µM (Fig. S3B). Therefore, 2 µM was served as the optimal concentration for preparation of CDs-DNA walker. Subsequently, to obtain the optimal detection performance of miRNA-21, the volume of CDs-DNA walker was studied. Experimental results showed that the ECL intensity reached the saturation when the volume added to 5 μL, which was utilized as the optimal volume of CDs-DNA walker for subsequent experiments (Fig. S3C). Next, the different metal ions as cofactors were evaluated to explore the optimal catalytic activity of DNAzyme. We find that the Mn2+ was a stronger catalytic activity than other metal ions (Fig. S3D). Thus, Mn2+ was chosen as the cofactor for operation of the DNAzyme.

In order to achieve efficient detection of dual targets, the incubation time of CDs-DNA walker/miRNA-21 covered on the electrode was optimized. As presented in Fig. S4A, the ECL intensity increased distinctly with the incubation time from 20 min to 120 min and then barely changed after 120 min. This result indicated that the incubation time of 120 min was long enough to ensure complete CP1/miRNA-21/CP2 hybridization. Therefore, the optimized incubation time of miRNA-21, CDs-DNA walker was 120 min in this study. The effect of pH value on the hybridization efficiency between miRNA-21, CP1 and CP2 was also studied (Fig. S4B), and found that when the pH value is 8, the intensity of ECL was the highest. In other words, the efficiency of miRNA-21, CP1, and CP2 hybridization was the best, which was used as optimal pH value for the following experiments. We further examined the effect of DNA-walking reaction time and Mn2+ concentration on the sensing of miRNA-155. The ECL intensity of the sensing system decreased with the extension of walking time within 60 min, and then slowly decreased (Fig. S4C). Therefore, 60 min was selected for the DNA walker reaction. And the best signal was obtained when the concentration of Mn2+ was 0.75 mM Mn2+ (Fig. S4D). As a consequence, 60 min and 0.75 mM Mn2+ were chosen as the best conditions for the walking process in the following study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical ability of the ECL biosensor in miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the proposed ECL biosensor was first used to detect miRNA-21. The ECL intensity gradually increased with an increasing in the concentration of miRNA-21 from 0 to 100 nM ( Fig. 2A) and showed a good linear relationship with the logarithm of the miRNA-21 concentrations ranging from 100 fM to 100 nM (Fig. 2B). The limit of detection (LOD) was 33 fM calculated as 3SB/m, where SB and m are the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 20) and the slope of calibration curve at lower concentration ranges, respectively. In the case of miRNA-155, the ECL signal gradually decreased with the increasing miRNA-155 concentration in the range from 0 to 100 pM (Fig. 2C) and showed a good linear relationship with the logarithm of the miRNA-155 concentrations ranging from 100 aM to 100 pM (Fig. 2D). The detection limit was down to 33 aM according to 3SB/m. The sensitivity of miRNA-21 was lower than that of miRNA-155 may be due to the steric hindrance effect of the SNA. By comparing with other reported methods ( Table 1), our ECL biosensor exhibited broader linear ranges as well as lower detection limits for miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 determination. The high sensitivity and wider detection range of this method can be ascribed to the efficient amplifying the microRNA signals by DNA walker, inherent high sensitivity and large dynamic range of ECL detection technique.

In addition to sensitivity, the selectivity of the biosensor was also investigated in the presence of potential interfering RNAs containing miRNA-141 and miRNA-182 (Fig. S5). As expected, no significant ECL signal change was observed except those incubated with miRNA-21 or miRNA-155 despite the fact that the concentration of interfering counterparts is 100 times. Therefore, the new biosensor has demonstrated an extraordinary specificity toward miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 when tested against other miRNAs individually or as a mixture.

Furthermore, the reproducibility of the biosensor was examined responses to 10 pM miRNA-21 and 1 pM miRNA-155 by comparing the ECL of five electrodes fabricated at different batches, respectively. As seen in Fig. S6A, the RSD was calculated to be 1.37% for miRNA-21 and 1.83% for miRNA-155, indicating a good reproducibility of the developed ECL biosensor.

Stability is one of the major concerns for practical application of biosensors. Fig. S6B displayed the ECL emission of the biosensor for detection of 100 pM miRNA-21 after being stored 14 days at 4 °C. The ECL intensity were quite stable and retained > 95% of its initial value, indicating a satisfying stability of the constructed biosensor.

To demonstrate the feasibility of a more practical application of our biosensor, the detection of miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 from human serum and the total RNA extraction solution from the human cancer cell line (Hela and MCF-7) was performed. The serum samples were collected from local hospital, 10-fold diluted with PBS and spiked with different amounts of miRNAs. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries of miRNA-21 and miRNA-155 were 96.0–102.0% and 96.4–103.0%, with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.9–4.4% and 3.2–4.1%, respectively, demonstrated that the potential application of the fabricated biosensor in human serum.

Then two kinds of human cancer cell lines were applied for evaluation the biosensor’s applicability. The cell culture and total RNA extraction are shown in the Supporting Information. As seen in Fig. 3A, miRNA-21 was expressed at a particularly low level in Hela cells and at a high level in MCF-7 cells, while miRNA-155 was overexpressed in MCF-7 instead of Hela, which are consistent with the reported studies [48–50].

In summary, we have developed a “on-off” ECL biosensor for detection of dual miRNAs that uses a single ECL luminophore as reporter molecule and employs the DNA walker to amplify the signal. In contrast to conventional multisignal mode-sensing method, the biosensor using a single ECL luminophore can effectively avoid the problems of signal overlap, the complexity of optimizing labeling conditions, and the limited number of labels available for multiplex assays. Moreover, the combination of DNA walker and ECL assay technique endows the method with high sensitivity, achieving the detection limits of 33 fM for miRNA-21 and 33 aM for miRNA-155, respectively. In addition, the proposed biosensor allowed efficient detection of multiple miRNAs in 10-fold diluted human serum samples and the total RNA extraction solution from the human cancer cell line (MCF-7), offering new perspectives in practical applications for biomolecular analysis and clinical diagnosis.

Li Wang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Kai-Ren Zhao: Data curation, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Zhi-Jun Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yi-Bang Zhang: Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Peng-Fei Liu: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Shu-Ying Ye: Writing – review & editing. Ye-Wang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Guoxi Liang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Advanced Talents Science Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052 and 10JDG038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130660.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Wang received her MS degree and Ph.D degree from ShaanXi Normal University. She is currently working at Department of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. Her current research interests include multifunctional nanomaterial and their applications in diagnosis, metastasis, and treatment of cancer.

Kai-Ren Zhao is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.

Zhi-Jun Liu is presently pursuing her master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

Yi-Bang Zhang received his MS degree from Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics, Chinese Academy of Sciences and Ph.D degree from Academy of Military Medical Sciences. He is currently working at Department of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. His current research interests include lipid nanovesicle technology and its medical application, artificial biofilm and drug screening evaluation system and development and mechanism of transdermal immune technology.

Peng-Fei Liu is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Shu-Ying Ye presently pursuing her master degree in environmental engineering in Jiangsu University. Her research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.

Ye-Wang Zhang received his MS degree from Dalian University of Technology and Ph.D degree from East China University of Science and Technology. He is currently working at Department of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. His current research interests include new technology development of biological production of important pharmaceutical intermediates，screening and preparation of important biocatalysts and pharmaceutical process integration technology.

Guo-Xi Liang received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University. He is currently working at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. His current research interests include nanobiosensor and nanofabrication.
